key_insight: A split-head quantum GAN can separately model lattice bounds and atomic positions, improving structural diversity while preserving physically meaningful crystalline generation.
engineering_relevance: This architecture offers a resource-efficient path for discovering novel crystalline materials by combining quantum feature mapping with physics-informed design priors.
method: Use a quantum trunk with a split-head design to decouple cell-level lattice parameters from atomic coordinates.
Compare SH-QGAN against an architecture-matched classical ablation to isolate the effect of quantum circuits.
Evaluate generation on the constrained Mg-Mn-O materials system for thermodynamic and geometric validity.
evidence_signal: On Mg-Mn-O, the classical ablation achieved better thermodynamic precision, while SH-QGAN more than doubled geometric validity and produced novel metastable candidates near Mg2MnO4 stoichiometry.
watch_item: Results suggest the quantum model boosts diversity, but performance depends on careful architecture matching and the limited near-term capacity of quantum hardware.
paper_title: Split-Head Quantum Generative Adversarial Network for Crystalline Material Discovery
